note_data.title: Inheritance
keyPoints: **Inheritance** is the passing on of features from parents to offspring through genes
**Genes** are sections of DNA that control the development of characteristics
**DNA** is found in the nucleus of cells and carries genetic information
**Chromosomes** are structures made of DNA and proteins that carry genes
**Mitosis** produces two diploid cells that are genetically identical to the parent cell
**Meiosis** produces four haploid gametes that are genetically different from each other
**Mutations** are random changes in genes or chromosomes that may be inherited
Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes (46 total) in diploid cells and 23 chromosomes in haploid gametes
Sex is determined by X and Y chromosomes - females are XX, males are XY

definitions.definition: A section of DNA that controls the development of a characteristic
A structure made of DNA and proteins that carries genes
Nuclear division that produces two genetically identical diploid cells
Nuclear division that produces four genetically different haploid gametes
A random change in genes or chromosomes that may be inherited

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give similarities AND differences between mitosis and meiosis
Give detailed account of process - include stages and what happens

modelAnswer.question: Explain why meiosis is important for sexual reproduction. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Meiosis produces gametes that are haploid, so when fertilisation occurs the diploid number is restored. Meiosis produces genetic variation in the gametes through crossing over and independent assortment. This genetic variation is important for survival of the species as it increases chances of survival in changing environments.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Produces haploid gametes / restores diploid number at fertilisation
Mark 2: Produces genetic variation / crossing over / independent assortment
Mark 3: Genetic variation important for survival / adaptation

commonMistakes: Never say 'genes are inherited' - always say 'characteristics are inherited through genes'
Students confuse mitosis and meiosis - mitosis is for growth, meiosis is for gamete formation
Don't say 'DNA controls characteristics' - say 'genes control characteristics'
Never say mutations are always harmful - they can be beneficial, harmful or neutral
Students forget that humans are diploid (2n=46) but gametes are haploid (n=23)
examinerTips: This topic appears almost every year in Paper 2, especially mitosis vs meiosis comparison
Always state the number of cells produced AND their genetic makeup for mitosis/meiosis questions
Use the terms diploid and haploid correctly - diploid = 2 sets, haploid = 1 set
Remember to mention crossing over when explaining genetic variation in meiosis
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Diagram showing mitosis producing 2 identical diploid cells vs meiosis producing 4 different haploid gametes, with chromosome numbers labeled
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2022 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to use pedigree information to conclude X-linked recessive inheritance pattern and provide evidence
This topic appeared in O/N 2023 P1 Q8 (3 marks) — students had to construct genetic diagrams for sex-linked inheritance showing expected phenotypic ratios
This topic was examined in M/J 2021 P2 Q5 (8 marks) — candidates needed to deduce complementary gene interaction from unusual offspring ratios like 15:1
This topic featured in O/N 2022 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — students analyzed Y-linked inheritance patterns using pedigree evidence

workedExample.question: Use the information in Fig. 3.1 to make one other conclusion about the pattern of inheritance of X-linked protoporphyria. Give evidence to support your conclusion. (3 marks)
steps: Step 1: Examine the pedigree diagram and identify affected individuals and their parents
Step 2: Look for patterns - note that affected individuals (shown as filled symbols) appear to have unaffected parents (shown as empty symbols)
Step 3: Apply genetic principles - if affected individuals have unaffected parents, the allele must be recessive
Step 4: State conclusion clearly and provide specific evidence from the diagram
workedExample.answer: The inheritance is recessive. Evidence: affected individuals have unaffected parents (e.g. individual III-2 is affected but both parents II-1 and II-2 are unaffected).
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for stating 'recessive', 2 marks for correct evidence citing specific individuals from pedigree showing affected offspring from unaffected parents

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused dominant and recessive inheritance patterns' — always check if affected individuals can have unaffected parents (recessive) or if every affected individual has at least one affected parent (dominant)

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to show gametes in genetic diagrams' — always include a clear gametes row showing all possible allele combinations before constructing your Punnett square

Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates often failed to distinguish between genotype and phenotype in their answers' — use proper genetic notation (e.g. XᵃXᵃ) for genotypes and descriptive terms (e.g. affected female) for phenotypes
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must master drawing genetic diagrams (Punnett squares) showing: parental genotypes with clear notation (e.g. XᴬXᵃ × XᵃY), gametes row showing all possible combinations, 2×2 or larger grid with offspring genotypes, and phenotypic ratio clearly stated. For sex-linkage, always show X and Y chromosomes with superscript alleles.
note_data.quickRecap: Inheritance patterns: dominant (affected parents have affected children), recessive (unaffected parents can have affected children), sex-linked (affects males more than females). Always construct genetic diagrams with parental genotypes, gametes, Punnett square, and phenotypic ratios. Use pedigree evidence to support conclusions about inheritance patterns.
